title: How to Work with Grief That Continues After a Psychedelic Journey
body:
How to Work with Grief That Continues After a Psychedelic Journey

So, you’ve just come back from a psychedelic journey and it hit you hard. Maybe you expected healing, some breakthrough, or clarity. Instead, you’re still stuck with grief. Maybe it even feels heavier. Know what I mean? That ache that doesn’t fade, that lump in your throat that won’t go away. This isn’t unusual. Psychedelic experiences can stir up grief that’s been buried or reveal new layers you didn’t see before.

Here’s the thing, grief isn’t a problem you fix with one trip. It’s a process, sometimes messy, often nonlinear. If you’re wondering how to handle grief that lingers after your journey, you’re in the right place. We’ll walk through how to work with that grief without trying to push it away or pretend it’s gone.

Why Grief Can Persist After Psychedelics

First, let’s clear up a common misconception: psychedelics don’t erase pain. They can open doors to parts of your psyche that were locked tight, but sometimes that means confronting grief that’s been on pause for years. Maybe you lost a loved one recently, or it’s a grief you never really allowed yourself to feel. The trip might have peeled back layers, and now you’re exposed to raw feelings that need attention.

Truth is, psychedelics often spark a process rather than deliver a final solution. You might have experienced moments of clarity or compassion with yourself, but grief is stubborn. It can show up again and again. That’s normal. What’s not normal is trying to shove it down because you think you should be “healed” by now.

Grief is a wound that calls for care over time. If you don’t give it that care, it festers. If you do, it can transform, but on its own schedule.

Step 1: Acknowledge Where You’re At

This sounds simple, but it’s powerful. Sit with your grief without judgment. Don’t tell yourself you should be over it because of your psychedelic experience. If you feel anger, sadness, loneliness, or confusion - say it out loud or write it down. “I’m feeling lost right now. I miss them deeply.” It doesn’t have to be pretty or poetic.

Try naming the grief. For example, if you lost your mom two years ago, say: “This grief is for Mom.” If it’s a breakup or a missed opportunity, name that too. Naming helps you stop running from the feelings.

One technique that’s helped many people is journaling with prompts like:

  • What does this grief feel like in my body right now?
  • What am I afraid will happen if I fully feel this?
  • What do I need to tell the person or situation I’m grieving?

You don’t have to share this with anyone. Let it be your safe space. This is a foundational step, so don’t rush it. Give yourself permission to just be with the pain.

Step 2: Track Your Physical Signals

Grief isn’t only emotional. It has a physical footprint. Maybe your chest feels tight, your stomach churns, or your energy crashes. Pay attention to these signals. Some days, your body might be screaming for rest. Other days, it might want movement.

Here’s a concrete tool: try tracking your Heart Rate Variability (HRV). It’s a measure of your nervous system’s resilience. When grief is intense, your HRV often dips, signaling your system is stressed. I recommend a WHOOP HRV monitor (paid link) for this. It’s discreet, works 24/7, and gives you real data to understand your stress and recovery patterns.

When your HRV is low, it’s a sign to slow down. No pushing through workouts or “tough it out” mentality. The body needs to heal too. This awareness can prevent burnout, which often sneaks up during grief.

Step 3: Ground Yourself with Routine and Ritual

Grief can make time feel slippery. Days might blend or stretch endlessly. So, creating small routines can anchor you. It doesn’t have to be complicated. Something like:

  • Drinking a warm cup of tea in the morning while sitting by a window
  • Taking a 10-minute walk outside, noticing your breath and surroundings
  • Lighting a candle or placing a photo of your loved one somewhere you see daily

Routines give structure when grief threatens to overwhelm. Rituals add intention and respect for the process. One of my students, Maya, shared this: after her trip where she encountered grief for her father, she started writing letters to him every evening. Not to send, just to express. It gave her relief and a sense of connection.

Find what works for you. Maybe it’s planting a small garden, or reading a book that helps you feel less alone. Speaking of books, if you want to understand more about altered states and how they relate to grief and healing, I suggest Stealing Fire (paid link). It’s not just about psychedelics but about accessing vital states of consciousness that support healing.

Step 4: Don’t Do It Alone - Find Support That Fits

Look, grief can isolate you, even if you’re surrounded by people. After a psychedelic journey, that isolation can deepen because others might not understand what you’re going through. You might feel like you’re “supposed” to be fine or “enlightened” now.

That’s a trap. You need people who see you, not who expect you to perform. This might be a therapist, a trusted friend, or a support group. If you’re in the psychedelic space, finding an integration coach who’s skilled in grief work can help you sort through what came up after your trip.

If you’re unsure where to start, try local grief groups or online communities specifically for psychedelic integration. Sharing your story can take some weight off your shoulders. I’ve written more about how to build integration practices after your trip that might give you some practical ideas.

Step 5: Move With Your Grief, Don’t Fight It

Sometimes grief feels like a tidal wave. You’re underwater, gasping for air. Other times, it’s a dull ache that just hums in the background. Both are okay. Don’t push yourself to “get over it” or “move on.” That’s not how grief works. It’s more like a dance where sometimes you’re leading, sometimes you’re following.

Movement can be a powerful ally here. This doesn’t have to mean intense exercise. Gentle yoga, walking in nature, or even shaking your body to release tension can help. When feelings get stuck in your tissues, movement helps them flow.

If you’re curious about natural supports that might help cognitive clarity and mood during grief, biohacking enthusiasts often recommend Lion's Mane mushroom capsules (paid link). People say it helps with focus and mental resilience, which can be useful when grief fog clouds your mind.

Step 6: Give Yourself Time and Permission to Feel Ambiguous

Grief after a psychedelic journey isn’t always straightforward. You might feel relief and sorrow in the same moment. That’s normal. You might feel more connected to the person you lost and also lonelier than ever. Don’t judge these feelings or try to “fix” contradictions.

One of my clients, Josh, told me about feeling “stuck between worlds” after his trip. He was grieving a friend who died but also felt an unexpected sense of peace. I told him, “Both can be true. You don’t have to pick sides.”

Allow the ambiguity. It means you’re processing honestly, not glossing over it. If you can hold space for that, the grief won’t consume you but will become part of your lived experience.

Step 7: Know When to Seek Professional Help

Sometimes grief can become overwhelming or lead to depression, anxiety, or even suicidal thoughts. If you find yourself in a dark place where daily functioning is impossible, reach out to a mental health professional right away. Psychedelic journeys can surface trauma that needs expert care.

It’s not a failure or weakness to ask for help. It’s a smart, brave step. Many therapists now specialize in integration and trauma-informed care for people who’ve done psychedelics.

For reliable information on grief and mental health, check out resources from Johns Hopkins Medicine. They offer clear guidance on when grief crosses into clinical territory.

Final Thoughts

Grief after a psychedelic journey is real and can be intense. You might feel like you’re caught in a loop or like the trip opened a wound that won’t close. That’s okay. Healing isn’t a straight path or a neat package. It’s sitting with discomfort, learning from it, and letting yourself be human.

If you want ongoing support, or just want to read more about living consciously with the ripples of psychedelic healing, visit me at kalesh.love. There you’ll find tools, essays, and community to help you through.

Remember: grief isn’t something you have to fix fast. It’s something you feel through, one day, one breath at a time. Does that land?

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
